summary:
Adam Smith's An Inquiry into the Nature and Causes of the Wealth of Nations (1776 AD) was the founding text of modern economics, a comprehensive analysis of how economies function that introduced the concepts of the division of labor, free markets, and the 'invisible hand' — the idea that individual self-interest, guided by market prices, leads to outcomes beneficial for society as a whole — that have been central to economic thought for 250 years. Smith, a Scottish moral philosopher at the University of Glasgow, had developed his economic ideas in lectures over decades; the Wealth of Nations was his comprehensive systematization, published in the same year as the [[event:2350|American Declaration of Independence]] and immediately recognized as a major work.

Smith's central arguments were simultaneously analytical and polemical. His famous opening example — the division of labor in a pin factory (where 10 workers dividing the 18 steps of pin-making produce 48,000 pins per day, while each working alone could make perhaps 20) — demonstrated that specialization and exchange were the sources of national wealth, not the accumulation of gold and silver (the mercantilist view he attacked). The 'invisible hand' (mentioned only once in the text) captured his insight that markets, through price signals, coordinate the dispersed knowledge of millions of individual actors more efficiently than any central planner could. His critique of mercantilism — the dominant economic policy of his era, which used trade restrictions and monopolies to favor domestic industries — was devastating, arguing that free trade produced benefits for all parties. Smith also recognized the costs of markets: his discussion of business's tendency to conspire against consumers and workers' interests ('People of the same trade seldom meet together... but the conversation ends in a conspiracy against the public') shows he was no naive celebrant of capitalism. The Wealth of Nations provided the intellectual foundation for the free-trade policies that dominated British economic policy in the 19th century and that shaped the [[event:108|Industrial Revolution's]] global spread.
